summary: Rule chunk from Gamemastering Paranoia page 98, chunk 4.

mechanics.raw_text:
To be honest, we've not met many players that can maintain this confident, com petent style consistently, but we've often ound it at least in flashes in even beginner groups and convention demos. This is one of the greatest pleasures of Paranoia — to con- front players with an impossible mission and watch them solve it anyway. * ‘Opinions vary on how to respond to this. Some recommend that you cheerfully slaughter PCs when they get too clever — 
nothing vicious or personal just a normal, in character response of The Computer and high- status NPCs who fear a competent competitor, Others recommend a judicious program of rewarding clever players with power, status and wealth — not too much too fast, but just ‘enough to permit them to take on ever-tougher assignments, Still others recommend that no special notice be given to the character (perhaps the greatest security in Alpha Com: pilex is remaining anonymous), but that the player be liberally praised when he enderuns ‘around the GM's most fiendish plots. Which ‘method is best? Who knows — we just don’t have that much experience with Stage Three players, Follow your instinets here. 3. Packaged Adventures vs. Home-Brew 4. Even inspited gamemasters like ourselves find lots of wonderful and entertaining ideas when we read published adventures. We cheertully steal these ideas and incorporate them into our own diabolical plots. 5. You can always take a published adventure, tse its basic structure or premise, then twist itin whatever direction you like. There are no ‘Game Design Police who come to your home to make sure you run an adventure the way it was published.
